ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for kothu prata. FOODON_03315595 (flatbread-based mixed dish) is the closest defensible parent category. A species-specific kothu prata term is absent from the ontology.
ontology.foodex2_description: Bread and bread products - mixed dish with meat
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for kothu prata. A0CKP (bread-based mixed dish with meat) is used as the closest defensible parent. Exact match unavailable.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for kothu prata mutton or a sufficiently close parent food concept. SNOMED CT anchor omitted.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of kothu prata mutton is approximately 350 g, typically served as a single plate at a hawker stall or restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Kothu Parotta (Mutton) — Nutrient composition estimated from ICMR-NIN component data: Parotta / Roti (plain), Mutton (cooked), Egg (whole, cooked), Onion, Tomato, and Cooking Oil
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, 2017 edition; supplemented by HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database for contextual validation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN or HPB entry exists for kothu prata mutton as a composite dish. Values are estimated by weighted ingredient composition: approximately 45% roti prata (flatbread), 30% cooked mutton, 10% egg, 8% onion and tomato, 7% cooking oil and spices. ICMR-NIN 2017 values used for each component. The resulting composite is consistent with HPB Singapore data for similar Indian mixed flatbread dishes. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of flatbread-based mixed dishes — analogous estimate based on roti / parotta GI data; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for kothu prata mutton. GI estimated at 62 (medium) based on the GI of plain parotta/roti (GI ~62, University of Sydney GI Database) as the dominant carbohydrate source (~45% of dish weight). The presence of mutton protein and egg reduces the effective GI relative to plain parotta. GL per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (62 × 75.25) / 100 ≈ 46.7, rounded to 46.8. Available carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 21.5 × 3.5 = 75.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~1330 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit kothu prata mutton to occasional consumption and request reduced salt preparation, as a single serving can contribute approximately 55–65% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~62) and high glycemic load per serving (~47)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume kothu prata mutton in moderated portions, as the high glycemic load per standard serving can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; pairing with non-starchy vegetables and limiting portion to half a plate may help attenuate the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~3.9 g per 100 g) and sodium (~380 mg per 100 g) from oil, mutton, and cooking method
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of kothu prata mutton consumption due to its moderate saturated fat and high sodium content per serving, and should prefer preparations using less oil and leaner cuts of mutton to reduce atherogenic risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kothu Prata Mutton is a popular Indian-Singaporean hawker dish made by chopping and stir-frying shredded roti prata (a flaky South Indian flatbread) together with tender pieces of cooked mutton, beaten egg, sliced onions, diced tomatoes, and a blend of aromatic spices on a hot iron griddle. The dish originates from the South Indian kothu parotta tradition and has been adapted in Singapore's multicultural food culture, where it is commonly served at Indian Muslim and mamak-style eateries. The texture is hearty and slightly chewy from the prata, with a rich, spiced flavour profile from the mutton and masala seasoning. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g serving provides approximately 763 kcal, 35.7 g protein, 37.8 g fat, and 75.3 g carbohydrate, making it a calorie-dense, protein-rich meal. The sodium content is high (~1330 mg per serving), which is clinically relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The medium glycemic index (~62) and high glycemic load (~47 per serving) make portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is generally considered halal and is a staple in Singapore's Indian Muslim food scene. It is best consumed as an occasional meal rather than a daily staple due to its energy density and sodium load.
common_user_queries: How many calories in kothu prata mutton?
Is kothu prata good for diabetics?
What is the glycemic index of kothu prata?
Is kothu prata high in sodium?
Difference between kothu prata and roti prata

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for kothu prata mutton as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN 2017 component data for roti/parotta, cooked mutton, egg, onion, tomato, and oil using weighted ingredient proportions. GI is an analogous estimate based on parotta GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high because no direct measurement of this composite dish was found in any approved primary source.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN 2017 component data; no single approved-source entry exists for kothu prata mutton as a prepared composite dish. 2. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN component sources used for this estimation. 3. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN component sources used for this estimation. 4. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN component sources used for this estimation. 5. GI is an analogous estimate based on plain parotta/roti GI (~62) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for kothu prata mutton was found. 6. The dish is classified as Indian-Singaporean reflecting its South Indian origin and Singaporean hawker adaptation. 7. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are closest-parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for this dish. 8. Halal status is inferred from the typical preparation context at Indian Muslim eateries in Singapore; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 9. Serving size of 350 g is estimated based on typical hawker portion sizes for this dish category in Singapore.
